title: Big Data & Data Architectures (Lambda/Kappa, Lake vs Warehouse)
body:
**Big Data** is usually characterized by the **V's**: Volume, Velocity, Variety (structured/semi-structured/unstructured), plus Veracity and Value. This variety is exactly why one relational engine no longer fits every workload — the course's central thread is: *given a scenario, pick the right storage technology and justify it* (a recurring exam exercise: "which technology is best for this ER model / this access pattern, and why?").

**Structured vs semi-structured vs unstructured.** Relational data is *structured* (fixed schema, tables). **Semi-structured** data (JSON, XML, graphs) has a self-describing but flexible schema — the natural fit for document/graph/key-value stores. **Unstructured** data (text, images, video) has no explicit schema and needs specialized indexing (inverted indexes for text, embeddings/ANN for vector search).

**Data lake vs data warehouse.** A **data warehouse** stores *structured, cleaned* data, schema is defined **on write** (ETL: extract-transform-load), optimized for fast, well-known analytical (OLAP) queries — high query performance, low flexibility. A **data lake** stores *raw* data of any format at low cost, schema is applied **on read** (ELT: extract-load-transform), maximizing flexibility for exploratory/unknown future use cases at the price of query performance and governance. A **lakehouse** tries to combine both: lake storage with warehouse-like transactional/schema guarantees on top.

**Lambda architecture.** Splits processing into three layers to reconcile *low latency* with *accuracy*: a **batch layer** (stores the full immutable dataset, recomputes accurate batch views periodically, e.g. via Hadoop/Spark), a **speed layer** (processes only recent data with low latency, e.g. via a stream engine, producing approximate real-time views), and a **serving layer** (merges batch + speed views to answer queries). Drawback: **two codebases** doing similar logic (batch and stream) must be kept in sync — extra complexity.

**Kappa architecture.** Simplifies Lambda by treating **everything as a stream**: a single stream-processing pipeline (append-only log, e.g. Kafka) handles both real-time and historical processing (reprocessing = replaying the log from the start). One codebase, but the streaming engine must support replay and enough throughput/state to substitute for batch.

**Exam angle.** Be ready to justify choosing lake vs warehouse for a scenario (is the schema known upfront? is the priority flexibility or query speed?), and to compare Lambda vs Kappa (why Kappa avoids the dual-codebase problem, when Lambda's separate accurate batch view is still preferable).

title: ER & Relational Model Recap
body:
Most exams open with an **ER diagram** (entities, attributes, relationships, generalization/specialization hierarchies with sub-types like `Person → Paramedic/Firefighter/Policeman`) and then ask you to **pick a storage technology** for parts of it and translate the model. Refresh the essentials so this translation step is fast:

- **Entities** → relational tables with a primary key; **1:N relationships** → foreign key on the "N" side; **N:M relationships** → a junction/bridge table holding both foreign keys (plus relationship attributes); **generalization hierarchies** map to either one table per subtype, one table for the whole hierarchy with a discriminator column, or a hybrid.
- **Normalization** (1NF/2NF/3NF) removes redundancy and update anomalies but adds **joins** — the classic trade-off you're asked to reason about when moving to NoSQL, where **denormalization** (embedding, duplicating) is often preferred to avoid expensive joins/lookups.
- **When is relational still the right answer?** When data is naturally tabular, relationships are well-known and stable, strong consistency/**ACID** transactions matter, and query patterns require ad-hoc joins/aggregations (SQL). This is the baseline the exam expects you to contrast against every NoSQL alternative.

**Exam angle.** The very first exercise of most exams is "which entities/relationships would you store in which DB technology, and why" — anchor your answer in: cardinality/volume (huge & growing → columnar), relationship-heavy & traversal-heavy (→ graph), nested/document-shaped & queried as a whole (→ document), simple lookups by key with TTL (→ key-value), free-text search/ranking (→ search engine), and default to relational only when none of those match better.

title: NoSQL Foundations: CAP Theorem & BASE
body:
**Why NoSQL.** Relational DBs struggle with horizontal **scalability** (sharding relational data while preserving joins/ACID is hard), schema rigidity for **semi-structured** data, and very high write throughput. NoSQL trades some relational guarantees for **horizontal scalability**, **schema flexibility**, and performance at scale.

**CAP theorem.** In a distributed system you can only guarantee **two of three** at once:
- **Consistency (C)** — every read receives the most recent write (or an error).
- **Availability (A)** — every request receives a (non-error) response, without guaranteeing it's the latest write.
- **Partition tolerance (P)** — the system keeps operating despite network partitions (dropped/delayed messages between nodes).

Since network partitions **will** happen in any real distributed system, **P is mandatory**, so the real-world trade-off is **C vs A** under a partition: **CP** systems (e.g. MongoDB by default, HBase) refuse/queue requests to preserve consistency; **AP** systems (e.g. Cassandra, DynamoDB) keep answering but may return stale data, reconciling later. **CA** without P only exists on a single node / non-partitioned setup.

**ACID vs BASE.** Relational DBs target **ACID** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) — strong guarantees, poor horizontal scalability. Many NoSQL stores instead target **BASE**: **B**asically **A**vailable, **S**oft state (state may change over time even without input, as replicas converge), **E**ventual consistency (if no new updates occur, all replicas eventually converge to the same value). BASE sacrifices immediate consistency for availability and partition tolerance — the AP corner of CAP.

**Consistency models spectrum.** From strongest to weakest: **strong/linearizable** (reads always see the latest committed write) → **eventual** (replicas converge given enough time, no ordering guarantee in between) → tunable variants like **read-your-writes**, **monotonic reads**, **causal consistency**. Cassandra exposes this as a **tunable consistency level per query** (see the columnar section).

**Exam angle.** True/false questions love CAP subtleties: "a system can guarantee all of C, A, and P simultaneously" is **false**; "eventual consistency guarantees replicas will converge if no new writes occur" is **true**; know which named systems are CP vs AP by default and *why* (their write/replication strategy).

title: MongoDB: Document Model & Schema Design
body:
**Document model.** MongoDB stores data as **BSON documents** (binary JSON) grouped into **collections** (no fixed schema is enforced by the engine, though your application/exam answer should still describe one, coherent with the ER model). A document is a set of field–value pairs; values can be scalars, arrays, or **nested (embedded) sub-documents**.

**Embedding vs referencing — the core design decision** (asked almost every exam, e.g. "how many collections would you define, and how would you implement the relations?"):
- **Embed** when the child is always accessed together with the parent, has a bounded/small cardinality, and doesn't need independent existence (e.g. an `Address` inside a `Person`, or one-to-few children). Embedding avoids joins (`$lookup`) at read time — MongoDB's main performance argument.
- **Reference** (store the related document's `_id` and look it up, similarly to a foreign key) when the child is large, shared across many parents, updated independently, or the relationship is one-to-many/many-to-many with unbounded growth (e.g. `Earthquake` → many `Flooding` events it caused).
- A hierarchy like `Natural Disaster → Earthquake/Flooding/Landslide/Tornado` is naturally modeled as **one collection per concrete subtype** (or one collection with a `type` discriminator field), embedding shared attributes and subtype-specific fields together per document.

**Document example:**
```json
{
  "_id": ObjectId("..."),
  "type": "Earthquake",
  "magnitude": 8.4,
  "location_id": ObjectId("..."),
  "causedFloodings": [
    { "description": "very strong earthquake", "landslides": [ { "...": "..." }, { "...": "..." } ] }
  ]
}
```

**Exam angle.** Justify the number of collections from access patterns ("queried together" → embed / same collection; "queried independently or huge" → separate collection + reference), and be explicit about which field holds the reference.

title: MongoDB: Query Language & Aggregation Pipeline
Neo4j: Property Graph Model
body:
**Property graph model.** Data is **nodes** (entities, each with one or more **labels** and a set of key-value properties) connected by **relationships** (directed, typed, also carrying properties). Unlike the relational model, relationships are **first-class, materialized** — traversing them is a pointer-chase, not a join, so graph DBs excel at deep/variable-length relationship queries (recommendation, path-finding, "friends of friends") that would need many self-joins in SQL.

**Modeling from an ER diagram** (a recurring exercise: "sketch a graph model for these entities"): entities → node labels; entity attributes → node properties; relationships → typed edges, with the relationship's own attributes stored as edge properties (e.g. `Drive` between `Person` and `Vehicle`, or `Rate` with a `score` property between `User` and `Movie`). Hierarchies (`Person → Paramedic`) can be modeled with **multiple labels on one node** (`:Person:Paramedic`) rather than separate node types — a key modeling difference from relational sub-typing.

Example sketch:
```
(:Person {name}) -[:DRIVES]-> (:Vehicle {plate})
(:Firefighter:Person {name}) -[:DRIVES]-> (:FireTruck:Vehicle {waterCapacity})
```

**Exam angle.** When asked to "sketch a graph model," show node labels + key properties + relationship types + direction, and note where a hierarchy collapses into multi-labeled nodes.

title: Neo4j: Cypher Query Patterns
body:
**Cypher** is a declarative, pattern-matching query language — you draw the graph shape you want with ASCII-art-like patterns.

**Core clauses:** `MATCH` (pattern to find), `WHERE` (filter), `WITH` (pipe/aggregate intermediate results to the next part of the query, like a SQL subquery boundary), `RETURN`, `ORDER BY`, `LIMIT`. Pattern syntax: `(n:Label {prop: value})-[:REL_TYPE]->(m:Label)`; `-[:REL*1..5]-` for variable-length paths; `-[:REL]-` (no arrow) for either direction.

**Aggregation** uses `count()`, `collect()`, `sum()`, `avg()` inside `RETURN`/`WITH`, implicitly grouping by the other returned non-aggregated variables (there's no explicit `GROUP BY` — whatever isn't aggregated becomes the grouping key).

**Worked pattern** — "count people who drive ≥5 vehicles, one being an Ambulance or FireTruck":
```cypher
MATCH (p:Person)-[:DRIVES]->(v:Vehicle)
WITH p, collect(DISTINCT v) AS vehicles
WHERE size(vehicles) >= 5
  AND any(v IN vehicles WHERE v:Ambulance OR v:FireTruck)
RETURN count(p) AS total
```

**Worked pattern** — "Fire Trucks with capacity > 100L driven by ≥5 Firefighters named John, each of whom also drove 10 other non-FireTruck vehicles":
```cypher
MATCH (f:FireTruck)<-[:DRIVES]-(p:Firefighter {name: "John"})
WHERE f.waterCapacity > 100
WITH f, p
MATCH (p)-[:DRIVES]->(other:Vehicle)
WHERE NOT other:FireTruck
WITH f, p, count(DISTINCT other) AS otherVehicles
WHERE otherVehicles >= 10
WITH f, count(DISTINCT p) AS firefighters
WHERE firefighters >= 5
RETURN f
```

**Common traps.** Use `WITH` to "checkpoint" and re-aggregate across multiple conditions that each need their own `count(DISTINCT ...)` — cramming everything into one `MATCH`+`WHERE` often double-counts or under-counts because of how pattern matching multiplies rows. `count(DISTINCT x)` vs `count(x)` matters whenever a pattern can match the same node via multiple paths. Filtering on a node's *label* uses `n:Label`, not a property comparison.

title: Key-Value Stores: Redis
body:
**Model.** The simplest NoSQL model: a big distributed hash map of `key → value`, with **O(1)** lookup by key and no query language beyond key access — you sacrifice ad-hoc querying for raw speed. Redis keeps data **in-memory** (optionally persisted to disk), making it ideal for **caching**, session stores, counters, rate limiting, leaderboards, and pub/sub messaging.

**Data structures beyond plain strings** — this is what Redis exams actually probe: **Strings**, **Lists** (ordered, allow duplicates), **Sets** (unordered, unique), **Sorted Sets/ZSets** (unique members ranked by a score — leaderboards), **Hashes** (a field→value map under one key, like a mini-document).

**Core commands (exam style):**
```
SET best_book "La ragazza delle Arance" EX 100    # key-value with TTL (seconds)
TTL best_book                                      # check remaining TTL
PERSIST best_book                                  # remove TTL -> permanent

RPUSH Farm "Bessy" "Bob"                           # create/append to a list
LPUSH Farm "Bessy"                                 # push to the front
LPOS Farm "Bob"                                    # find position (or SISMEMBER for a set)

SET Dog "Jerry" EX 1000                            # new key expiring in 1000s

SADD "HP Books" "Harry Potter and the Philosopher Stone"
SISMEMBER "HP Books" "Harry Potter and the Deadly Hallows"   # returns 0/1
```

**Exam angle.** Know the exact command for each structure: `SET ... EX <seconds>` for TTL at creation, `TTL key` to inspect it, `PERSIST key` to strip it, `RPUSH`/`LPUSH` for lists, `SADD`/`SISMEMBER` for sets. Redis is the default answer whenever the scenario says "fast lookup by a single key," "cache," "session," or "expire after N seconds" (TTL) — that phrase is the giveaway for key-value over any other NoSQL model.

title: Columnar DB: Cassandra Data Model
body:
**When to pick columnar.** The default justification in exams: **large-scale, continuously/rapidly growing, write-heavy** data (e.g. sensor readings, sales/transaction logs) where you need horizontal write scalability and mostly query by a known key pattern rather than ad-hoc joins.

**Model.** Cassandra organizes data into **column families (tables)** inside a **keyspace** (≈ a database, where you also configure the **replication strategy/factor**). Unlike a relational table, Cassandra tables are **denormalized and query-driven**: you design the table around the queries you need to run, not around normalized entities — duplication across tables is expected and preferred over joins (Cassandra has **no joins**).

**Primary key = partition key + clustering columns.**
```sql
CREATE KEYSPACE market WITH replication = {'class': 'SimpleStrategy', 'replication_factor': 3};
USE market;

CREATE TABLE sales_by_market (
  market_id   text,
  sale_time   timestamp,
  product_id  text,
  farmer_id   text,
  price       double,
  PRIMARY KEY ((market_id), sale_time, product_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (sale_time DESC);
```
- The **partition key** (`market_id`, in parentheses) determines which node(s) store the row (via a hash) — all rows sharing it live in the same partition, enabling fast reads for that key **and** distributing write load across the cluster as new partitions are created.
- The **clustering columns** (`sale_time, product_id`) order rows *within* a partition on disk, enabling efficient range scans (e.g. "most recent sales first") without scanning the whole partition.
- Choosing the partition key is *the* design decision: too coarse → hot partitions (bad write distribution); too fine → can't do the range queries you need. This is exactly why "large, continuously increasing" data pushes you to pick a partition key like `market_id` or a time-bucket, not a global auto-increment id.

**Query.**
```sql
SELECT * FROM sales_by_market WHERE market_id = 'M1' AND sale_time > '2024-01-01';
SELECT * FROM probes WHERE version = 'v1.1.0' ALLOW FILTERING;  -- non-key predicate: needs ALLOW FILTERING (or a secondary index) and is a full scan warning sign
```

**Exam angle.** You're usually asked to (a) write `CREATE KEYSPACE`/`USE`/`CREATE TABLE` faithful to the ER model, choosing a sensible partition/clustering key for the stated access pattern, then (b) write a `SELECT` — remember queries **must** filter by the full partition key (equality) before any clustering-column range filter; filtering on a non-key column needs `ALLOW FILTERING` (flag this as inefficient in a written answer).

title: Cassandra: Replication & Tunable Consistency
body:
Cassandra is the textbook **AP** system (peer-to-peer, masterless ring, no single point of failure) exposing **tunable consistency per query** rather than one fixed level — this is the practical illustration of the CAP trade-off from the NoSQL-foundations section.

**Replication factor (RF)** = how many nodes hold a copy of each partition. **Consistency level (CL)** = how many replicas must acknowledge a read/write before it's considered successful, independent of RF:
- `ONE` — fastest, least consistent (only 1 replica must respond).
- `QUORUM` — a majority of replicas ($\lceil RF/2 \rceil + 1$... more precisely $\lfloor RF/2 \rfloor + 1$) must respond — the common balance.
- `ALL` — every replica must respond — strongest consistency, worst availability (fails if any replica is down).

**Strong consistency condition:** if $R$ = read CL and $W$ = write CL, then $R + W > RF$ guarantees the read set and write set overlap in at least one replica, so a read always sees the latest write (e.g. `QUORUM`/`QUORUM` with RF=3 gives $2+2=4>3$).

**Exam angle.** Given RF and CL values, determine whether the setup guarantees strong consistency ($R+W>RF$?) or only eventual consistency, and reason about the availability cost of each choice (e.g. `ALL` reads/writes fail entirely if one replica in the partition is unreachable, breaking the "partition tolerance" promise in practice).

title: Data Warehousing & Snowflake
body:
**Dimensional modeling.** A data warehouse is organized around a central **fact table** (numeric, additive measures — e.g. `sale_amount`, `quantity` — one row per business event) surrounded by **dimension tables** (descriptive attributes for slicing/filtering — `Time`, `Product`, `Customer`, `Store`).

- **Star schema** — dimension tables are fully denormalized (flat), directly joined to the fact table: fewer joins, faster queries, some redundancy.
- **Snowflake schema** — dimensions are further normalized into sub-dimension tables (e.g. `Product → Category → Department`): less redundancy, more joins, slightly slower queries. The choice is a storage/redundancy vs query-simplicity trade-off, the OLAP analogue of the normalization trade-off from the relational section.

**ETL vs ELT.** Classic warehousing uses **ETL** (extract from sources, transform/clean/conform to the target schema *before* loading) — schema-on-write, strong governance, but rigid and slow to add new sources. Modern cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery) favor **ELT**: load raw data first, transform inside the warehouse using its compute — leverages cheap elastic cloud compute/storage and is more flexible, closer to the data-lake philosophy while retaining SQL and ACID-like guarantees on structured data.

**Snowflake architecture** separates **storage** and **compute**: data sits in cheap object storage, and independent, elastically-scalable **virtual warehouses (compute clusters)** run queries against it — multiple workloads can query the same data concurrently without contending for the same compute, and you scale each independently (pay only for compute used).

**OLTP vs OLAP.** OLTP (operational relational DBs) — many small read/write transactions, normalized schema. **OLAP** (data warehouse) — few, large, read-mostly analytical queries (aggregations, `GROUP BY`, `ROLLUP`) over a denormalized, historical, dimensional schema.

**Exam angle.** Distinguish star vs snowflake by counting/describing the join depth to a dimension; explain why cloud warehouses lean ELT; contrast OLTP vs OLAP schema and query shape.

title: Information Retrieval Foundations & Elasticsearch Mapping
Elasticsearch Query DSL
Hadoop: HDFS & MapReduce
body:
**HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System).** Splits large files into fixed-size **blocks** (typically 128MB) distributed across a cluster's **DataNodes**, with a **NameNode** holding the metadata (which blocks belong to which file, where each block replica lives). Each block is **replicated** (default factor 3) across different nodes/racks for fault tolerance and to enable **data locality** (schedule computation where the data already resides, avoiding network transfer — a core Big Data principle: *move computation to the data, not data to computation*).

**MapReduce programming model.** A batch job is expressed as two user-defined functions over key-value pairs:
- **Map(k1, v1) → list(k2, v2)** — runs in parallel on each input split (often one per HDFS block), transforming/filtering records independently.
- **Shuffle & sort** (framework-managed) — groups all Map outputs by key $k2$ and sends each group to a Reducer.
- **Reduce(k2, list(v2)) → list(v3)** — aggregates all values for a given key.

**Canonical word-count reasoning** (the pattern every Hadoop exam question reduces to): Map emits `(word, 1)` for every word in a line; Reduce sums the 1's per word. Generalize this shape to any "group by X, aggregate Y" exam question: identify what becomes the **map's output key** (the grouping field) and what the **reduce** aggregation is (sum/count/max/concat).

**Combiner** — an optional local "mini-reduce" run right after Map on each node, pre-aggregating before the network shuffle, cutting shuffle traffic (valid only for associative/commutative aggregations like sum/max, not e.g. computing an average directly — that needs sum+count carried separately).

**Exam angle.** Given a scenario, state Map's output `(key, value)` shape and Reduce's aggregation logic; recognize when a combiner is safe to add (associative & commutative ops) and when it changes semantics (e.g. average requires emitting `(sum, count)` pairs, not raw values, so the reducer can finish the division).

title: Apache Spark: RDDs, DataFrames & Spark SQL
body:
**Why Spark over MapReduce.** Spark keeps intermediate data **in memory** across stages (rather than writing to disk between every Map/Reduce step) and builds a **DAG** of operations, giving large speedups for iterative/multi-stage workloads (ML training, interactive queries) — the standard exam contrast with Hadoop's MapReduce.

**RDDs (Resilient Distributed Datasets).** Immutable, partitioned collections processed with two operation kinds:
- **Transformations** (`map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `reduceByKey`, `groupByKey`, `join`) — **lazy**: build up the DAG but compute nothing yet.
- **Actions** (`collect`, `count`, `reduce`, `saveAsTextFile`) — **trigger actual execution** of the whole accumulated DAG.
This laziness lets Spark optimize the whole pipeline before running it (e.g. pushing filters early, fusing map stages) — remember: nothing happens until an action is called.

**DataFrames & Spark SQL.** A **DataFrame** is a distributed table with a schema (columns + types) built on top of RDDs, offering a higher-level, more optimized API (via the Catalyst optimizer) than raw RDD transformations. **Spark SQL** lets you register DataFrames as temporary views and query them with standard SQL, or use the equivalent DataFrame API (`.select()`, `.filter()`, `.groupBy().agg()`, `.join()`).

**Worked pattern** — "total number of original films directed by directors whose nationality equals the film's language":
```sql
SELECT COUNT(*)
FROM Director d
JOIN Direct dr ON d.Personal_ID = dr.Director_ID
JOIN Film f ON dr.Film_ID = f.Film_ID
WHERE d.Nationality = f.Language
  AND f.Original = true;
```

**Worked pattern** — "total budget of films with 'Curse' in the title, directed by directors born after a date, grouped per director":
```sql
SELECT d.Personal_ID, SUM(f.Budget) AS total_budget
FROM Director d
JOIN Direct dr ON d.Personal_ID = dr.Director_ID
JOIN Film f ON dr.Film_ID = f.Film_ID
WHERE f.Title LIKE '%Curse%'
  AND d.Birth_Date > '1994-09-08'
GROUP BY d.Personal_ID;
```

**Exam angle.** Spark SQL questions are essentially standard SQL over the ER model — the Spark-specific knowledge tested is *why* Spark is used (in-memory DAG execution, lazy transformations vs eager actions) rather than exotic syntax; write correct joins/`GROUP BY`/`HAVING` and you're covering what's graded.

title: Vector Databases: Embeddings & Similarity Search
body:
**Why vector DBs.** Traditional indexes (B-trees, inverted indexes) answer exact/range/keyword queries; they can't answer "find items *semantically similar* to this one." Vector databases (e.g. Milvus/Zilliz, Pinecone, Weaviate) index high-dimensional **embedding vectors** (numeric representations produced by an ML model, e.g. capturing semantic similarity of text/images) and answer **nearest-neighbor** queries.

**Similarity search.** Given a query vector, find the $k$ stored vectors closest to it by a distance/similarity metric: **Euclidean (L2) distance**, **cosine similarity** $\left(\cos\theta = \frac{u\cdot v}{\|u\|\|v\|}\right)$, or **inner (dot) product**. Exact nearest-neighbor search is $O(n)$ per query (compare against every stored vector) — too slow at scale.

**Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) indexes.** Trade a small amount of recall for large speedups: **IVF** (inverted file — cluster vectors, search only the nearest clusters), **HNSW** (hierarchical navigable small-world graphs — greedy graph traversal toward the query), **product quantization** (compress vectors to reduce memory/compute). Vector DBs let you choose the index type and its accuracy/speed trade-off per collection.

**Query pattern (Milvus/Zilliz-style):**
```python
results = client.search(
    collection_name="pilot_collection",
    data=[query_embedding],
    search_params={"metric_type": "COSINE", "params": {"nprobe": 10}},
    filter='name like "Mic%"',
    output_fields=["championships_won"],
    limit=5
)
```
Typical exam completion tasks ask you to fill in: the **collection name**, the **query vector(s)** (`data`), a **scalar filter expression** alongside the vector search (hybrid filtering — combining an exact/string condition with the similarity search), and `limit`/`top_k`. Post-processing (grouping the returned hits by a field and counting, e.g. "how many pilots per number of championships") is then done in plain Python/Pandas on the result set, mirroring the aggregation patterns from MongoDB/SQL.

**Exam angle.** Know that a vector DB is chosen when the query is "most similar to X" rather than an exact/range predicate, that similarity search is inherently approximate at scale (ANN), and be comfortable completing a partially-given hybrid (vector + scalar filter) query.

title: Feature Stores
body:
**The problem.** ML teams recompute the same features (aggregates, joins, transformations over raw data) separately for **training** (offline, batch, historical) and **serving** (online, low-latency, real-time) pipelines — duplicated logic risks **training/serving skew**: subtly different feature values at train vs inference time silently degrading model quality.

**Feature store role.** A centralized system that computes features **once** and serves them consistently to both paths:
- **Offline store** — large-scale, batch-computed features (e.g. backed by a data warehouse/lake) used to build training datasets with **point-in-time correctness** (join features as they were known *at the time* of each training example, avoiding label leakage from future data).
- **Online store** — a low-latency key-value-style store (fast lookups by entity id, e.g. user_id) serving the *current* feature values to a live model at inference time.
- A **feature registry** documents feature definitions/metadata, enabling reuse and discovery across teams/models instead of every project recomputing its own version of "average purchase amount last 30 days."

**Why it fits this course.** The online store is architecturally a **key-value store** (Redis-like, fast point lookups) and the offline store behaves like a **data warehouse/lake** (batch, historical, point-in-time joins) — the feature store is a concrete example of composing the storage technologies covered elsewhere in the course for a specific ML-serving use case.

**Exam angle.** Explain training/serving skew and point-in-time correctness, and identify which storage paradigm (KV vs warehouse/lake) backs the online vs offline path and why (latency requirement vs historical-batch requirement).

title: Data Wrangling
body:
**Data wrangling (munging)** is the process of transforming raw, messy data into a clean, well-structured form ready for analysis/storage — the practical bridge between "data as collected" and any of the storage/query technologies covered elsewhere in the course.

**Typical pipeline stages:**
- **Discovery** — understand the source data's structure, quality, and semantics before touching it.
- **Structuring** — reshape data into the target schema (e.g. pivoting wide↔long, flattening nested JSON into tabular form or vice-versa when moving relational data into a document model).
- **Cleaning** — handle missing values (imputation, removal), remove duplicates, fix inconsistent formats/units, correct outliers/errors.
- **Enriching** — augment with additional derived or external data (computed features, joined reference data).
- **Validating** — check consistency/quality rules (types, ranges, referential integrity) programmatically.
- **Publishing** — deliver the cleaned dataset to its destination (a warehouse, a document store, a training pipeline).

**Connection to schema-on-write vs schema-on-read.** Warehouses (schema-on-write) push most wrangling *before* load (classic ETL); lakes (schema-on-read) defer structuring/cleaning until query time (ELT) — wrangling is the same set of operations, just relocated in the pipeline depending on the target architecture.

**Exam angle.** Given a messy/raw dataset description, identify which wrangling stage(s) apply and connect the transformation needed to the target storage model (e.g. "flatten this nested structure" when moving into a relational table vs "keep nested, just validate types" when moving into MongoDB).
